item.title: Issue #2742 created
item.description:
JNode identity: closer alignment with XML node identity?

A JNode wraps a map or array as the root of a JTree if fn:jtree is called, or if a path is traversed. fn:jtree currently says that it “typically returns a new JNode with its own identity”, but “may” return the same JNode for repeated calls on the same input; the behavior for queries like (1 to 3) ! {} ! jtree() (which may be rewritten to replicate({}, 3) ! jtree()) is left to the implementation.

The sheer length of the rules and notes for fn:jtree indicates that it is prone to unexpected system behavior (something I already experienced multiple times now during development). For instance, the behavior of the example given for fn:distinct-ordered-nodes…

let $x := [ 1, "a", true() ]
return count(distinct-ordered-nodes(
  ($x/*[1], $x/jnode(*, xs:integer)) 
))

…is currently implementation-defined (and needs to be dropped or changed if we keep the current rule set for fn:jtree).

We have already discussed this before, but my impression is that the current state is not satisfying enough to keep it. Maybe we should think about strengthening the rule so that every explicit/implicit construction of a root JNode yields a JNode with a distinct identity. The advantages I see:

  1. Same model as for XML: XDM gives every constructed node a fresh identity, and processors already preserve this under optimization. Reusing the same rule would allow us to reuse much more existing XNode properties and optimizations.
  2. Identity independent of value: JNode identity depends on the map/array’s weakly-defined value identity. Tying it to the construction instead means value optimizations (sharing, folding, etc.) could never change a result.
  3. Simplicity: I would assume that simpler rules automatically improve the consistence of implementations and (at least in the long term) makes it easier for users to understand what’s going on.

The arising challenge will be how to tackle the increased nondeterminism of ·jtree· and the path expression, but it’s something that is at least known from functions like ·fn:parse-xml·.
